title: TypeScript Fundamentals
summary: Types, type inference, union/intersection types, and the narrowing mental model that makes TypeScript safe.

questions.prompt: TypeScript types exist at runtime and can be checked with instanceof:
choices: True — TS compiles types into runtime metadata
False — types are erased at compile time; only JS class constructors can use instanceof
questions.explanation: TypeScript is structurally erased. Interfaces, type aliases, unions — none exist at runtime. Only JavaScript constructs (class, typeof, in) work at runtime.

questions.prompt: What type does TypeScript infer for `result`?
questions.explanation: TypeScript infers an array of the union of all literal types present in the literal. A tuple type [number, string, boolean] would only be inferred with `as const`.

questions.prompt: What is the type of `PI` after `const PI = 3.14`?
questions.explanation: `const` causes TypeScript to infer the literal type `3.14` rather than the widened type `number`. `let PI = 3.14` would infer `number`.

questions.prompt: What does TypeScript report on line 3?
choices: Error on line 2: Type 'number' is not assignable to type 'string'
Error on line 3: name is undefined
questions.explanation: TypeScript inferred `name: string` from the string literal. Assigning `42` (a number) violates the inferred contract — error on line 2, not line 3.

questions.prompt: `unknown` differs from `any` in that `unknown`:
choices: Must be narrowed before performing operations on it
Is only for function return types
Is the same as any but slower
questions.explanation: Both accept any value. The difference is `any` turns off checking; `unknown` keeps it on — you must prove the type (via typeof, instanceof, type guard) before calling methods.

questions.prompt: What happens?
choices: Compiles fine — unknown is compatible with string
TypeScript compile error: Property 'toUpperCase' does not exist on type 'unknown'
Works if x is actually a string at runtime
questions.explanation: `unknown` blocks all method access until narrowed. You must first do `if (typeof x === 'string')` before calling string methods.

questions.prompt: Given `type A = { x: number } & { y: string }`, a value of type A:
choices: Can have either x or y
Must have both x: number and y: string
Is only valid if x equals y
Is the same as { x: number } | { y: string }
questions.explanation: Intersection `&` means ALL properties from both sides are required. It is more restrictive than union `|`, which means AT LEAST ONE side.

questions.prompt: What is the return type of a function that always throws an error?
questions.explanation: `never` means the function never successfully returns. It is different from `void` (returns but with no value). `never` signals unreachable / always-throws.

questions.prompt: Which narrowing guards are valid in TypeScript?
choices: x is string (in regular code, not a type guard function)
questions.explanation: `x is string` is only valid as the return type annotation of a type predicate function, not as a standalone expression. The other three are standard JavaScript runtime checks TypeScript recognises.

questions.prompt: What is the type of `config.port` after `const config = { port: 3000 } as const`?
questions.code:
const config = { port: 3000 } as const;
type T = typeof config.port;
questions.explanation: `as const` freezes all values to their literal types. `config.port` becomes the literal type `3000`, not the wide type `number`. The object is also readonly.

questions.prompt: Why does TypeScript widen `let x = 'north'` to `string` but not `const x = 'north'`?
choices: let is slower so TS uses a simpler type
let allows reassignment so the value could change — any string is valid. const cannot be reassigned so the literal is locked.
This is a bug in TypeScript
questions.explanation: TypeScript widens `let` to the base type because you could later write `x = 'south'` — keeping the literal type would immediately break. `const` guarantees the value never changes so the literal type is safe.

questions.prompt: `typeof null` in JavaScript (and therefore TypeScript at runtime) evaluates to:
questions.explanation: This is a famous JavaScript bug that TypeScript inherits. `typeof null === 'object'`, so you must ALSO check `x !== null` when narrowing objects. `null` is NOT an object in TS's type system but is at runtime.

questions.prompt: What does TypeScript infer for return type?
questions.explanation: TypeScript infers the return type from the body. `number + number` is `number`, so the return type is inferred as `number` — no annotation needed.

questions.prompt: Which TypeScript feature acts as a compile-time exhaustiveness check for switch statements?
choices: Assigning the default variable to type never — TS errors if a case is unhandled
questions.explanation: After all union members are handled, what remains is `never`. Assigning it to a `never`-typed variable is harmless if exhaustive — but if you add a new union member later without a case, TS will error with 'not assignable to never'.

questions.prompt: A custom type guard function returns `x is string`. When TypeScript sees this function return true:
choices: It trusts the JavaScript runtime result
It narrows x to string in the true branch of the caller
It ignores the return type — only typeof works
It checks the assertion at runtime
questions.explanation: A type predicate `x is string` tells TypeScript: 'wherever you see this function return true, narrow the argument to string'. It is a compile-time contract, not a runtime check.

questions.prompt: Which of these are erased at compile time (do not exist in the JS output)?
questions.explanation: Interfaces and type aliases exist only at compile time. Class declarations ARE emitted as JavaScript (they are runtime values). Union annotations are purely compile-time.

questions.prompt: After this code, what is the type of `items`?
questions.explanation: The `as string[]` assertion overrides the default inference of `never[]` for an empty array literal. Without the assertion, TS infers `never[]` because it has no evidence of element type.

questions.prompt: What does enabling `strict: true` in tsconfig.json actually enable?
choices: A set of flags including strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, strictFunctionTypes, and others
questions.explanation: `strict: true` is a shorthand that enables: strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, strictFunctionTypes, strictBindCallApply, strictPropertyInitialization, noImplicitThis, and alwaysStrict. It is the recommended default.

questions.prompt: Why does `let x: any = 5; x.toUpperCase()` not produce a TypeScript error but crashes at runtime?
choices: any disables all type checking — TS trusts you completely, so no static error is raised even for invalid operations
questions.explanation: `any` is an escape hatch that tells TypeScript 'I know what I'm doing'. It disables every check. The blame at runtime is yours, not TypeScript's — this is why `any` should be avoided.

questions.prompt: What is the type of `x` inside the else branch?
questions.explanation: After ruling out string and number, only boolean remains. TypeScript's narrowing tracks what has been eliminated — the else branch gets boolean, not the full union.

flashcards.back: `let x = 'a'` infers `string` (widened). `const x = 'a'` infers `'a'` (literal). Use const for config values you never reassign.
Both accept any value. `unknown` requires narrowing before use. `any` skips all checks. Default to `unknown` for external data; never use `any` in new code.

flashcards.front: never type — when does it appear?
flashcards.back: Functions that always throw, exhaustive switch defaults, and intersection of incompatible types. Use it as the sentinel in exhaustiveness checks.
`typeof`, `instanceof`, `in`, `Array.isArray`, custom type predicate (`x is T`). TypeScript tracks each guard's effect on the type in each branch.
Freezes all values to their literal types and makes the object deeply readonly. Use for config objects, lookup tables, and discriminated union tag arrays.
`typeof null === 'object'` — JavaScript legacy bug. Always pair an object check with `x !== null` when narrowing nullable references.
strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, strictFunctionTypes, strictBindCallApply, strictPropertyInitialization, noImplicitThis. Enable from day one — retrofitting strict is painful.
`A | B` = either A or B (OR). `A & B` = both A and B (AND). Unions widen; intersections constrain. Narrow unions before calling type-specific methods.

project.brief: Design the type system for a small API client library in TypeScript. The library fetches user data from a REST endpoint that can return: a User object, an Error object, or null (not found). Define: 1) the union type for all three response shapes, 2) a type guard function for each case, 3) a processResponse function that uses narrowing to handle each case without any `any`. Write out the types, guards, and function signature — no implementation needed. Then explain: why is `unknown` the right type for the raw JSON before parsing? What would break if you used `any` instead?

checklist.text: Defines a discriminated union or tagged union for the three response shapes
Writes a type guard (x is User, x is ApiError) for each shape
processResponse is fully typed with no any or non-null assertions
Explains why unknown is safer than any for raw JSON input
Identifies what breaks if any is used (missed null case, wrong method calls)

hints: A discriminated union uses a shared literal field (like `type: 'user' | 'error' | 'not-found'`) so narrowing is predictable.
A type guard function returns `x is User` — TypeScript narrows in the true branch of every caller.
unknown accepted BUT operations blocked: you must parse/validate before calling .name or .message.
any accepted AND all operations allowed: a typo like `.nme` compiles fine, explodes at runtime.
